One major purpose of Christ’s coming was to set aside the ceremonial law; and to make way for this, he rejects the ceremonies people added to the law God made. The clean hands and pure heart that Christ gives to his disciples, and requires of them, are very different from the outward and superstitious practices of Pharisees in every age. Jesus rebukes them for rejecting the commandment of God. It is clear that it is the duty of children, if their parents are poor, to help them as far as they can; and if children deserve to die for cursing their parents, much more those who starve them. But if a man followed the traditions of the Pharisees, they found a way to excuse him from this responsibility.

Our wicked thoughts and affections, words and actions, defile us, and these alone. As a corrupt spring sends forth corrupt streams, so does a corrupt heart send forth corrupt reasonings, corrupt appetites and passions, and all the evil words and actions that come from them. A spiritual understanding of the law of God, and a sense of the evil of sin, will cause a man to seek the grace of the Holy Spirit, to keep down the evil thoughts and affections that work within.

Christ never turned away anyone who fell at his feet, which a poor trembling soul may do. As she was a good woman, so she was a good mother. This led her to Christ. His saying, ‘Let the children first be filled,’ shows that there was mercy for the Gentiles, and it was not far off. She spoke, not as if making light of the mercy, but praising the abundance of miraculous cures among the Jews, in comparison with which a single cure was only like a crumb. Thus, while proud Pharisees are left by the blessed Savior, he shows his compassion to poor humbled sinners who look to him for children’s bread. He still goes about to seek and save the lost.

Here is a healing of one who was deaf and dumb. Those who brought this poor man to Christ asked him to observe the case and use his power. Our Lord used more outward actions in doing this healing than usual. These were only signs of Christ’s power to heal the man, to encourage his faith, and that of those who brought him. Though we find great variety in the cases and manner of relief for those who applied to Christ, all obtained the relief they sought. This is still true in the important matters of our souls.
